result:
When people say “write about whatever you want,” I always end up circling back to the same cluster of topics: how thinking works, why we tell stories, and how much of what we consider “solid” in our lives is actually fragile, contingent, improvized in the moment. So I’ll do that here—loosely, informally—but I’ll try to keep it grounded in things that you can actually notice in your own experience rather than drifting into pure abstraction.

I’m going to move through a few themes:

1. How minds improvise reality  
2. Why we’re so bad at introspection  
3. Stories as compression for experience  
4. The strange life of digital selves  
5. What “authenticity” even means now  
6. Some practical consequences of all this  

Not as a thesis, more as a walk through a series of connected rooms.

---

## 1. Minds improvise reality

It’s easy to think of perception as a camera and a microphone: the world is out there, it hits our senses, and we get a faithful feed. That picture is comfortable but wrong in almost every relevant way.

Your brain doesn’t passively receive the world; it actively predicts it, then patches errors. Neuroscientists sometimes call this “predictive processing.” The idea, very roughly, is:

- The brain constantly generates a model of what it expects to see, hear, feel.  
- Incoming sensory data are measured against this model.  
- The differences—the “prediction errors”—are used to adjust the model or, in some cases, to change your actions to make the world fit the prediction.

From the inside, it doesn’t feel like you’re predicting; it just feels like you’re perceiving. But you can catch the prediction machinery misfiring:

- When you mis-hear song lyrics and can’t un-hear the mistaken version for years.  
- When you think you see your friend in a crowd, only to realize the face is slightly wrong. Your brain filled in your friend’s features because “friend in this context” was a strong prediction.  
- When you see a typo in a sentence and still read it correctly because you “see” what you expect.

There’s a classic example: “THE CAT” written with the same ambiguous squiggle for the middle letter in both words. In “THE” you see an H; in “CAT” you see an A. The actual shape is identical; your brain resolves it differently depending on context.

This is not a small corner case—this is what perception is like all the time. Your experience is a negotiation between sensory data and a prediction engine trying to minimize surprise.

It has a lot of consequences.

### We don’t live in the same world

You and another person standing in the same room are not inhabiting the same perceptual reality. The raw photons and air vibrations are similar, sure, but:

- You predict different social risks.  
- You have different emotional associations with similar cues.  
- Your attention is drawn to different details—threats, opportunities, reminders.

Someone who grew up in a chaotic household may automatically scan for tension in voices and faces; someone who didn’t may barely notice micro-anger until it becomes overt. Someone who’s been badly embarrassed in public might experience any group conversation as a low-level threat environment; another might feel energized by it. The same “room” is a different world.

We tend to underestimate this. We say things like “I don’t know how they can believe that” or “how can they not see what’s right in front of them?” as if the obstacle is just stubbornness, not that the other person is actually perceiving a different constructed reality.

### Why uncertainty is so uncomfortable

The predictive brain hates not knowing where to land. Uncertainty isn’t just an intellectual state; it’s a physiological one. When your predictive model doesn’t know what to expect:

- Your arousal systems spike: elevated heart rate, vigilance, muscle tension.  
- You start searching for patterns—any pattern—because a wrong model is often less stressful than no model.

That’s why conspiracy theories are so attractive, particularly in times of social chaos: they’re “complete” models. They explain a lot, even if incorrectly, which for a prediction system is emotionally preferable to being adrift.

It’s also why big life transitions feel so raw. A breakup, a job loss, moving to a new city—they don’t just change your circumstances; they break a lot of your predictions. Your sense of “how tomorrow usually goes” stops working. You’re prediction-error all over the place.

One of the most stabilizing things you can do for yourself in those periods is explicitly reframe: “Of course this feels terrible. My predictive system is flooded, not just my ‘heart’ or ‘willpower.’ I need time to rebuild a working model.” That’s boring advice, but it makes the discomfort make sense.

---

## 2. Why we’re so bad at introspection

If the brain is mostly a predictive machine, why can’t it just tell you what it’s doing?

Because the part that tells “you” stories about yourself is not the part that runs the show.

If I ask you, “Why did you pick that shirt today?” you might say, “I like the color” or “It’s comfortable” or “It was on top of the drawer.” Those might be part of the story, but they’re not the complete causal chain. Dozens of unconscious influences played a role:

- What you wore last time and how people reacted.  
- Subtle weather cues, even before you consciously checked the forecast.  
- Half-remembered advice about what “looks good on you.”  
- A vague mood you woke up in that made certain textures feel more appealing.

The part of the brain that generates verbal explanations doesn’t have access to most of that. It’s like the spokesperson of a large, opaque organization: it comes out and says something plausible that sounds coherent.

Psychologists call the polished, verbal part the “interpreter.” When you ask it “why,” it doesn’t fetch a log of actual reasons; it invents a reasonable-seeming story that roughly matches your behavior and self-image. The scary part is: you nearly always believe it, because it’s the only story you hear.

This is why:

- We think we know our motives better than we do.  
- We confidently assert reasons for our choices that are empirically wrong (this is shown in experiments).  
- We are surprised by our own behavior when it contradicts our self-narrative, yet quickly spin that too into a new story.

Introspection is useful, but it’s more like examining a curated museum exhibit of yourself than like reading your source code.

### The limits show up under pressure

You can see this commentator-vs-actual-system divide in high-pressure moments.

Consider someone who’s socially anxious. Before walking into a party, their verbal mind says, “I know on a rational level nobody cares what I say, I’ll be fine.” Then they walk in, and their body response is: heart racing, sweating, over-analysis of every micro-reaction.

The spoken story (“I know this is fine”) is out of sync with the lower-level predictive and threat systems, which are using a different dataset—maybe middle school humiliation, maybe a parent who mocked them, maybe ten awkward events in a row. Telling yourself “nobody cares” doesn’t rewrite those predictions, because those systems don’t operate in verbal logic.

Therapy, gradual exposure, repeated experiences of “this goes differently now”—those are slow ways of updating the non-verbal systems so they stop flagging “party” as threat. Only then does the story “I’ll be fine” line up with bodily reality.

We often misdiagnose this as “weak will” instead of “mismatch between the story-layer and the deeper prediction machinery.”

---

## 3. Stories as compression for experience

Given that you’re living inside this high-dimensional tangle of perceptions, predictions, and mixed motives, you need a way to make it usable. Stories are one of the brain’s main compression algorithms.

A story takes:

- Thousands of sensory impressions,  
- A mess of half-understood motives,  
- Sequences of events with ambiguous causality,

and compresses them into:

> “That was the year I realized I can’t trust people like her.”  
> “After losing that job, I decided I’d never rely on anyone again.”  
> “I’m the kind of person who quits when things get hard.”

Those compressed narratives are incredibly efficient: you can carry them around for decades and use them to guide behavior in new contexts. They’re also dangerous, because compression always throws away information, often including exceptions and nuance.

### Stories stabilizing identities

A story like “I always mess things up” might have been originally formed to make sense of three or four painful episodes. Once established, it:

- Shapes where you look for evidence. You notice failures; you discount or forget successes.  
- Alters your behavior in subtle ways that increase the chances of fulfilling the story (self-sabotage, under-preparation, withdrawal).  
- Colors your future memories: even neutral events get narrated as “another time I proved I’m a screw-up.”

That story is not just a description; it’s an active force. Changing it is less like editing a paragraph and more like rewriting a key compression routine in your mental operating system.

This is one reason narrative-based therapy can be effective. You’re not just “talking about the past”; you’re literally inviting the brain to build new compressed versions of experiences, ones that highlight different aspects:

- From “I was humiliated and proved I’m worthless”  
- To “I was in an unfair environment with people who didn’t see me clearly”

Still a story, still compressed, but with different implications for future predictions and behavior.

### Stories as shared reality protocols

Stories also serve as synchronization tools between people. When we say we’re “on the same page,” we mean we’re using compatible narrative compressions.

A company might tell itself a story like: “We’re the scrappy underdog disrupting the incumbents.” That compresses thousands of decisions into:

- Move fast, take risks.  
- Forgive mistakes if they’re bold.  
- Define yourself against a big bad “them.”

Employees who buy into that story can coordinate without explicit instructions. They can guess what the “right” move would be in ambiguous situations because the story is their shared compression algorithm.

The problem arises when:

- Reality changes but the story doesn’t (the “underdog” becomes the market leader but still acts reckless).  
- Different subgroups have different compressions (“we care about craft and stability” vs. “we care about growth at all costs”).

Many ideological conflicts in politics are at least partly compression conflicts: different groups simplify the same complex history in incompatible ways, then act as if the other is stupid or evil for using their own compression algorithm.

---

## 4. The strange life of digital selves

Now put these storytelling, predictive brains into an environment where identity is increasingly mediated by digital platforms.

Online, you’re rewarded for being:

- Legible: quickly understood and categorized.  
- Consistent: predictable in your opinions and style.  
- Engaging: producing reactions (likes, comments, outrage).

Raw, full-spectrum human beings don’t naturally meet those criteria. We’re messy, ambivalent, context-dependent. So we sculpt digital selves that are more compressed and stylized.

Think of social media profiles as characters loosely based on you:

- The LinkedIn self: competent, upward-trending, inevitably “passionate” about something corporate-sounding.  
- The Instagram self: curated aesthetics, highlights of experience, taste display.  
- The Twitter/X self: hot takes, wit, ideological positioning.  
- The private chat self: more vulnerable, still filtered.

Each is a narrative compression tuned to a platform’s reward function. The platforms themselves are prediction machines: they estimate what will keep you scrolling, clicking, and sharing. There’s a resonance between:

- The brain’s predictive system shaping your behavior, and  
- The platform’s predictive system shaping your attention.

You end up in a feedback loop: what you do gets measured and rewarded or punished by algorithms; that response feeds your own predictions about “what works,” which shape your future actions online.

### Identity as an optimization problem

When people talk about “personal brands,” they’re usually describing an optimization problem: find a way of being that:

- Maximizes attention, status, or income,  
- While staying within the bounds of what you personally can tolerate.

This isn’t new—people have always managed impressions in villages, workplaces, and families. What’s new is:

- Scale: your potential audience can be enormous.  
- Persistence: posts hang around; old selves can resurface.  
- Metrics: you get quantitative feedback on your identity performance.

The temptation is to tune yourself toward whatever gets bigger numbers: more likes, more followers. In doing so, you can gradually move away from the messy, full-spectrum version of yourself toward a narrower, more caricatured one.

You feel this when someone becomes “the guy who always rants about X” or “the girl whose whole brand is being perpetually overwhelmed but quirky.” Their audience expects consistency; deviation is penalized. Over time, the character eats the person.

This can be exhausting. But walking away has its own cost: loss of social capital, income, or a sense of being seen. The trap is that even your resistance can become content: “burnout story,” “digital detox story,” another plotted arc for the character.

---

## 5. What authenticity even means now

The word “authenticity” gets thrown around constantly, usually as a marketing promise: brands want to look authentic, influencers want to claim authenticity, we’re told to “be our authentic selves.”

If you take seriously the ideas above—that perception is constructed, that introspective stories are incomplete, that we play multiple roles—it becomes hard to define authenticity as “just be the real you,” because:

- There isn’t a single, context-free “real you” waiting to be revealed.  
- Many of your motives and preferences are emergent from situations.  
- Your introspective stories about yourself are partial and often self-serving.

Still, we do have a sense that some ways of presenting ourselves feel more aligned and some feel more hollow or performative. That points toward a different, more workable definition of authenticity.

You might think of authenticity as:

> Coherence between what you present, what you actually feel and intend, and what you’re willing to own the consequences of.

A few parts to that:

1. **Presentation vs. felt experience**  
   - Authenticity doesn’t mean saying everything you think. Privacy and discretion are not inauthentic.  
   - But it does mean that what you *do* present is not in constant contradiction with your actual experience. If you’re miserable but relentlessly perform “I’m thriving” for months, something is off.

2. **Owning consequences**  
   - Sometimes we say things that look spontaneous but were actually optimized for approval. If the audience reaction turns, we might say “that’s not really me.”  
   - Authenticity in this sense means: if you say it, you’re prepared to say, “Yes, that was genuinely my view at the time; if it was flawed, I own that too.”

3. **Room for growth**  
   - Authenticity is not loyalty to your past self. Changing your mind publicly can be more authentic than doubling down on an outdated self-image.

This makes authenticity a continuous practice, not a fixed trait. It’s not “I am authentic” but “In this moment, to what extent am I aligning my presentation with my actual experience and values?”

### The cost of constant performance

When you routinely present a self that diverges too far from your inner experience, you create what psychologists sometimes call “self-discrepancy.” Many people feel it as:

- A low-level dread before posting or being seen.  
- A sense of being hollow in the middle of externally “good” moments.  
- Anxiety that people only like the character, not the person.

You can watch this play out with celebrities who become trapped in an early version of themselves that the public refuses to let them outgrow. But it’s not just them; anyone can get locked in:

- The “strong friend” who never admits weakness.  
- The “funny one” who doesn’t feel allowed to be serious.  
- The “always online” person who worries disappearing will make them irrelevant.

One corrective is deliberately reintroducing friction and slowness. Slower, smaller contexts where you don’t have to be legible to thousands of weak ties at once. More interactions that aren’t recorded, optimized, or scored. Spaces where you can experiment with being less compressed.

---

## 6. Practical consequences

All of this can sound theoretical, but there are some concrete ways it can matter in daily life. A few to close with.

### A. Treat your stories as hypotheses

When you catch yourself thinking in broad strokes—

- “I *always* do this”  
- “They’re *definitely* out to get me”  
- “This *proves* I’m not cut out for X”

—you’re seeing your brain’s compression at work. You don’t have to fight it with artificial positivity, but you can label it:

> “That’s one plausible story my brain is telling. What are two other possible compressions of these events?”

For example:

- Original story: “I bombed that presentation; I’m just not leadership material.”  
- Alternative: “I underestimated how much practice I needed for that setting; that’s a skill gap, not a fixed trait.”  
- Another: “I was exhausted and unprepared; the conditions sucked, not necessarily my potential.”

You’re not forcing yourself into delusion; you’re acknowledging that multiple compressed stories are compatible with the same messy reality. That looseness alone can reduce the pain.

### B. Notice prediction errors without panic

When something surprises you—someone behaves differently than you expected, a project that seemed sure-fire fails—it’s tempting to treat that as proof that you’re foolish or doomed.

From a predictive-brain viewpoint, surprise is just: “my model and the world disagreed.” The productive question is:

> “What did I implicitly predict, and what data might update that prediction?”

This re-frames mistakes from moral failures to information-rich events. You don’t have to like them, but you can mine them.

If a friendship ends abruptly, for example, instead of only narrating “people are untrustworthy” you might also ask, “What did I assume about their internal world that turned out wrong? Did I ignore signals? Over-interpret others?” The goal is gentler clarity, not self-blame.

### C. Guard some unoptimized spaces

Given how much of life is now mediated by algorithms, it helps to deliberately cultivate areas of your existence that are:

- Not recorded,  
- Not scored,  
- Not audience-shaped.

Examples:

- A hobby with no posting about it. You don’t take pictures of the thing; you just do it.  
- Time with a friend where phones are off the table and you’re not thinking in shareable snippets.  
- A private journal where you explicitly allow yourself to be inconsistent, petty, grandiose—all the things you’d edit out elsewhere.

These unoptimized zones give your predictive and storytelling systems somewhere to relax. They also help your sense of self not be entirely anchored to the compressed, metric-driven characters you play online.

### D. Be suspicious of totalizing narratives—especially about groups

When you feel a story like “These people are all X” sweep over you—whether “X” is stupid, evil, brainwashed, lazy—it’s worth pausing. That’s your brain reaching for maximal compression:

- Many individuals → a single, emotionally charged label.

Sometimes you do need shorthand; you can’t deeply understand every person or group. But when a narrative is convenient *and* emotionally satisfying, it’s especially liable to be distortive.

A useful question:

> “What data would I need to see to weaken this story even a little?”

You don’t have to abandon your view. Just recognizing that your story is not the only story, and that there could be counterexamples, helps prevent you from fully hardening into contempt.

### E. Allow your self-concept to be versioned

Think of “you” as a piece of software with versions rather than a statue made once and for all.

- “Me 1.0” might have been reactive, insecure, or naive.  
- “Me 2.0” might have learned certain skills, dropped others.  
- “Me 3.0” might be in progress right now.

The important part is: you’re allowed to deprecate old APIs. Pasts selves can be acknowledged without being eternal anchors.

Concretely, that can look like:

- “At that time, I sincerely held that view and acted from it. I no longer do, and I accept responsibility for the impact it had.”  
- “I used to think I was ‘just a person who doesn’t finish things.’ That was a compression based on a period of my life, not a lifelong sentence.”

Versioning yourself lets you benefit from compressed stories (“I used to be like this, now more like that”) without spending your whole life inside an outdated narrative.

---

All of this is, of course, another story—a compressed way of organizing a bunch of observations from neuroscience, psychology, and everyday life. It’s not the final word on minds, or reality, or digital culture. But it’s a workable lens:

- Your brain improvises reality with predictions.  
- It explains itself with partial, after-the-fact stories.  
- Those stories get compressed into identities.  
- Digital environments reward certain compressions over others.  
- Authenticity is less about discovering a hidden “real you” and more about aligning your stories, your actions, and your willingness to own them.

The value of thinking this way isn’t in being right in some ultimate sense. It’s in having a bit more flexibility: with yourself, with other people, and with the worlds—both physical and digital—that you move through every day.

When people talk about “personal brands,” they’re usually describing an optimization problem: find a way of being that:

- Maximizes attention, status, or income,  
- While staying within the bounds of what you personally can tolerate.

This isn’t new—people have always managed impressions in villages, workplaces, and families. What’s new is:

- Scale: your potential audience can be enormous.  
- Persistence: posts hang around; old selves can resurface.  
- Metrics: you get quantitative feedback on your identity performance.

The temptation is to tune yourself toward whatever gets bigger numbers: more likes, more followers. In doing so, you can gradually move away from the messy, full-spectrum version of yourself toward a narrower, more caricatured one.

You feel this when someone becomes “the guy who always rants about X” or “the girl whose whole brand is being perpetually overwhelmed but quirky.” Their audience expects consistency; deviation is penalized. Over time, the character eats the person.

This can be exhausting. But walking away has its own cost: loss of social capital, income, or a sense of being seen. The trap is that even your resistance can become content: “burnout story,” “digital detox story,” another plotted arc for the character.

---

## 5. What authenticity even means now

The word “authenticity” gets thrown around constantly, usually as a marketing promise: brands want to look authentic, influencers want to claim authenticity, we’re told to “be our authentic selves.”

If you take seriously the ideas above—that perception is constructed, that introspective stories are incomplete, that we play multiple roles—it becomes hard to define authenticity as “just be the real you,” because:

- There isn’t a single, context-free “real you” waiting to be revealed.  
- Many of your motives and preferences are emergent from situations.  
- Your introspective stories about yourself are partial and often self-serving.

Still, we do have a sense that some ways of presenting ourselves feel more aligned and some feel more hollow or performative. That points toward a different, more workable definition of authenticity.

You might think of authenticity as:

> Coherence between what you present, what you actually feel and intend, and what you’re willing to own the consequences of.

A few parts to that:

1. **Presentation vs. felt experience**  
   - Authenticity doesn’t mean saying everything you think. Privacy and discretion are not inauthentic.  
   - But it does mean that what you *do* present is not in constant contradiction with your actual experience. If you’re miserable but relentlessly perform “I’m thriving” for months, something is off.

2. **Owning consequences**  
   - Sometimes we say things that look spontaneous but were actually optimized for approval. If the audience reaction turns, we might say “that’s not really me.”  
   - Authenticity in this sense means: if you say it, you’re prepared to say, “Yes, that was genuinely my view at the time; if it was flawed, I own that too.”

3. **Room for growth**  
   - Authenticity is not loyalty to your past self. Changing your mind publicly can be more authentic than doubling down on an outdated self-image.

This makes authenticity a continuous practice, not a fixed trait. It’s not “I am authentic” but “In this moment, to what extent am I aligning my presentation with my actual experience and values?”

### The cost of constant performance

When you routinely present a self that diverges too far from your inner experience, you create what psychologists sometimes call “self-discrepancy.” Many people feel it as:

- A low-level dread before posting or being seen.  
- A sense of being hollow in the middle of externally “good” moments.  
- Anxiety that people only like the character, not the person.

You can watch this play out with celebrities who become trapped in an early version of themselves that the public refuses to let them outgrow. But it’s not just them; anyone can get locked in:

- The “strong friend” who never admits weakness.  
- The “funny one” who doesn’t feel allowed to be serious.  
- The “always online” person who worries disappearing will make them irrelevant.

One corrective is deliberately reintroducing friction and slowness. Slower, smaller contexts where you don’t have to be legible to thousands of weak ties at once. More interactions that aren’t recorded, optimized, or scored. Spaces where you can experiment with being less compressed.

---

## 6. Practical consequences

All of this can sound theoretical, but there are some concrete ways it can matter in daily life. A few to close with.

### A. Treat your stories as hypotheses

When you catch yourself thinking in broad strokes—

- “I *always* do this”  
- “They’re *definitely* out to get me”  
- “This *proves* I’m not cut out for X”

—you’re seeing your brain’s compression at work. You don’t have to fight it with artificial positivity, but you can label it:

> “That’s one plausible story my brain is telling. What are two other possible compressions of these events?”

For example:

- Original story: “I bombed that presentation; I’m just not leadership material.”  
- Alternative: “I underestimated how much practice I needed for that setting; that’s a skill gap, not a fixed trait.”  
- Another: “I was exhausted and unprepared; the conditions sucked, not necessarily my potential.”

You’re not forcing yourself into delusion; you’re acknowledging that multiple compressed stories are compatible with the same messy reality. That looseness alone can reduce the pain.

### B. Notice prediction errors without panic

When something surprises you—someone behaves differently than you expected, a project that seemed sure-fire fails—it’s tempting to treat that as proof that you’re foolish or doomed.

From a predictive-brain viewpoint, surprise is just: “my model and the world disagreed.” The productive question is:

> “What did I implicitly predict, and what data might update that prediction?”

This re-frames mistakes from moral failures to information-rich events. You don’t have to like them, but you can mine them.

If a friendship ends abruptly, for example, instead of only narrating “people are untrustworthy” you might also ask, “What did I assume about their internal world that turned out wrong? Did I ignore signals? Over-interpret others?” The goal is gentler clarity, not self-blame.

### C. Guard some unoptimized spaces

Given how much of life is now mediated by algorithms, it helps to deliberately cultivate areas of your existence that are:

- Not recorded,  
- Not scored,  
- Not audience-shaped.

Examples:

- A hobby with no posting about it. You don’t take pictures of the thing; you just do it.  
- Time with a friend where phones are off the table and you’re not thinking in shareable snippets.  
- A private journal where you explicitly allow yourself to be inconsistent, petty, grandiose—all the things you’d edit out elsewhere.

These unoptimized zones give your predictive and storytelling systems somewhere to relax. They also help your sense of self not be entirely anchored to the compressed, metric-driven characters you play online.

### D. Be suspicious of totalizing narratives—especially about groups

When you feel a story like “These people are all X” sweep over you—whether “X” is stupid, evil, brainwashed, lazy—it’s worth pausing. That’s your brain reaching for maximal compression:

- Many individuals → a single, emotionally charged label.

Sometimes you do need shorthand; you can’t deeply understand every person or group. But when a narrative is convenient *and* emotionally satisfying, it’s especially liable to be distortive.

A useful question:

> “What data would I need to see to weaken this story even a little?”

You don’t have to abandon your view. Just recognizing that your story is not the only story, and that there could be counterexamples, helps prevent you from fully hardening into contempt.

### E. Allow your self-concept to be versioned

Think of “you” as a piece of software with versions rather than a statue made once and for all.

- “Me 1.0” might have been reactive, insecure, or naive.  
- “Me 2.0” might have learned certain skills, dropped others.  
- “Me 3.0” might be in progress right now.

The important part is: you’re allowed to deprecate old APIs. Pasts selves can be acknowledged without being eternal anchors.

Concretely, that can look like:

- “At that time, I sincerely held that view and acted from it. I no longer do, and I accept responsibility for the impact it had.”  
- “I used to think I was ‘just a person who doesn’t finish things.’ That was a compression based on a period of my life, not a lifelong sentence.”

Versioning yourself lets you benefit from compressed stories (“I used to be like this, now more like that”) without spending your whole life inside an outdated narrative.

---

All of this is, of course, another story—a compressed way of organizing a bunch of observations from neuroscience, psychology, and everyday life. It’s not the final word on minds, or reality, or digital culture. But it’s a workable lens:

- Your brain improvises reality with predictions.  
- It explains itself with partial, after-the-fact stories.  
- Those stories get compressed into identities.  
- Digital environments reward certain compressions over others.  
- Authenticity is less about discovering a hidden “real you” and more about aligning your stories, your actions, and your willingness to own them.

The value of thinking this way isn’t in being right in some ultimate sense. It’s in having a bit more flexibility: with yourself, with other people, and with the worlds—both physical and digital—that you move through every day.
